coredata.dc:title: Intrinsic peroxidase activity of carbon dots derived from expired drugs and its application in automatic detection of total antioxidant capacity
coredata.dc:creator: Zhang, Guo-Qi
Liu, Wei-ping
Shi, Yu-Han
Zou, Yu-Xian
Xu, Li-Yao
Cheng, Hui-Ling
Wang, Tao
Li, Wen-Bin
Zhao, Yan
Xu, Zhi-Hong
coredata.dc:description: In the post-COVID-19 pandemic, the stockpiling of medicines by most households could pose a pollution risk as they may expire over time. In this study, an expired amoxicillin act as carbon source to prepare peroxide-like functional carbon dots nanozymes doped Fe (Fe-CDs). Importantly, a high-value treatment technique for expired drugs was proposed. To obtain outstanding catalytic performance, compared with other commonly doped metals, Fe-CDs catalyzed the oxidation of TMB by H2O2 with the highest efficiency. In particular, the oxidation product of TMB (oxTMB) would be alleviated in the presence of antioxidants. Significantly, antioxidants could briefly cause the fading of oxTMB and the fading level was correlated with various concentrations of antioxidants. Importantly, a sequential injection strategy was introduced to control the fading process caused by antioxidants to improve the accuracy. Under the optimum condition, the limit of detection (LOD) was 0.13 mg/L (for ascorbic acid) with the linear concentration range from 0.5 to 15 mg·L−1. Finally, the total antioxidant capacity (TAC) in human serum samples were evaluated successfully.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Fe-doping carbon dots
Expired waste drugs
Total antioxidant capacity
Like-peroxidase activity

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Enzyme-like catalytic property of the Me-CDs
Effect of reducing substance on Fe-CDs/H2O2/TMB reaction system
Analytical performance of Fe-CDs nanozyme for the detection of H2O2 and AsA
Application of the iSIS system to detect TAC based on the Fe-CDs nanozyme

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Sichuan Province Science and Technology Support Program

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: We appreciate the financial support from the Sichuan Science and Technology Program No. 2023NSFSC0637 and Xihua University Talent Introduction Program (No. RX2200001988). We would like to thank Shiyanjia Lab (www.shiyanjia.com) for the XPS and TEM testing and analysis.

We appreciate the financial support from the Sichuan Science and Technology Program No. 2023NSFSC0637 and Xihua University Talent Introduction Program (No. RX2200001988). We would like to thank Shiyanjia Lab (www.shiyanjia.com) for the XPS and TEM testing and analysis.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic diagram of the iSIS system for TAC.
The enzyme-like activity of Me-CDs. (A) POD-like for Me-CDs; (B) OXD-like for Me-CDs. (C) Effect of pH on POD-like and OXD-like properties of Me-CDs. M = Fe, CD, Co.

Effect of reducing substance on Fe-CDs/H2O2/TMB reaction system. (A) Absorbance spectra of reaction systems containing various reducing molecules, including AsA GSH and Cys. (B) Fe-CDs catalyzed H2O2 decomposition of ·OH and AsA inhibited the formation of ·OH were verified by ESR spectra with DMPO as the trapping agent. The AsA1 and AsA2 represented high and low concentrations(10 and 100 mg·L−1), respectively. The concentration of TMB, Fe-CDs, H2O2 were 0.5 g·L−1, 0.25 mg·mL−1, 5 mM, respectively. The reaction time was 5 min. The concentration of AsA, GSH and Cys were 10 mg·L−1, respectively.
The recording curves of the linearity of AsA determination. Insert is a standard curve for determining AsA.
Determination results of iSIS system based on the Fe-CDs nanozyme for TAC.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: *:The principle of total antioxidant capacity assay kit was ferric reducing ability of plasma.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Recovery results of AsA in dosages obtained by using the iSIS method.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: *The concentration values of samples and standards are after mixing by 1 + 1 ratio.

head.ce:title: Intrinsic peroxidase activity of carbon dots derived from expired drugs and its application in automatic detection of total antioxidant capacity

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Chemical Synthesis and Pollution Control Key Laboratory of Sichuan Province, China West Normal University, Nanchong 637000, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Chemical Synthesis and Pollution Control Key Laboratory of Sichuan Province

ce:affiliation.ce:label: e
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Clinical Laboratory, Zigong First People’s Hospital, Zigong 643000, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Clinical Laboratory, Zigong First People’s Hospital, Zigong 643000, China

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors have contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A automatic method for detection of total antioxidant capacity in human serum was established based on carbon dots nanozyme derived from expired drugs.

In the post-COVID-19 pandemic, the stockpiling of medicines by most households could pose a pollution risk as they may expire over time. In this study, an expired amoxicillin act as carbon source to prepare peroxide-like functional carbon dots nanozymes doped Fe (Fe-CDs). Importantly, a high-value treatment technique for expired drugs was proposed. To obtain outstanding catalytic performance, compared with other commonly doped metals, Fe-CDs catalyzed the oxidation of TMB by H2O2 with the highest efficiency. In particular, the oxidation product of TMB (oxTMB) would be alleviated in the presence of antioxidants. Significantly, antioxidants could briefly cause the fading of oxTMB and the fading level was correlated with various concentrations of antioxidants. Importantly, a sequential injection strategy was introduced to control the fading process caused by antioxidants to improve the accuracy. Under the optimum condition, the limit of detection (LOD) was 0.13 mg/L (for ascorbic acid) with the linear concentration range from 0.5 to 15 mg·L−1. Finally, the total antioxidant capacity (TAC) in human serum samples were evaluated successfully.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Many research showed that oxidative stress played a primary role in aging and in disease progression [1,2]. To gain a comprehensive understanding of oxidative stress in biological systems, it was essential to evaluate oxidative stress in vivo. Oxidative stress could be direct determination of oxygen/nitrogen species or simple quantified by indirect compounds of total antioxidant capacity (TAC), such as glutathione (GSH), cysteine (Cys), ascorbic acid (AsA), etc [3]. Other studies revealed that the TAC of in serum may has biological relevance to chronic diseases, for example diabetes, hypertension, Alzheimer, etc [4]. Therefore, it was necessary to develop a low-cost, high throughput and accurate analytical method for TAC in human serum.

To date, several commercially methods has been applied to evaluate the antioxidant activity, such as the oxygen radical absorbance capacity, ferric reducing antioxidant activity assay, 2,2-diphenyl-1-picrylhydrazyl assay. But in fact, these methods suffer from the detection of mercaptan insensitivity [5–7]. Benefiting from the unique properties of nanomaterials, the colorimetric method developed based on this could greatly improve the sensitivity. In recent years, a typical colorimetric assay for TAC was realized through inhibiting of catalyzed chromogenic products by antioxidants. In particular, the attractive analysis platform for TAC established by enzyme catalytic oxidation of 3,3′,5,5′-tetramethylbenzidine (TMB). Therefore, it was particularly important to develop various nanomaterials with like-enzyme properties, which were named as nanozymes. Firstly studies based on Yan et al. showed that Fe3O4 nanoparticles possessed intrinsic POD-like property to catalyze TMB in 2007 [8]. Since then, various nanozymes were used to catalyze TMB to detect TAC such as metal oxides [9], noble metal nanoparticles [10], carbon-based materials [11], etc. It has not only outstandingly catalytic activity, but also excellent tolerance that natural enzymes could not match. So nanozymes have great advantages in analyzing sensing. For instance, Huang et al. reported that Cu,N-codoped carbon nanosheets with specific peroxidase-like activity were applied for evaluating the TAC [12]. A portable colorimetric sensor for TAC based on Mn/Co oxides@carbon yolk-shell nanozymes were proposed by Yu group [13]. The single-atom iron anchored at carbon nanotube of nanozymes were designed for peroxidase mimicking, and it catalyzed the oxidation reaction of TMB to detect TAC [14]. Most nanozymes like the above nanozymes materials, however, were hydrophobic materials that raise the baseline in the spectral scan, which limited its wider application. Therefore, the excellent water solubility was likely a good promising way to develop nanozyme with inspiring homogeneous catalytic efficiency.

As the emerging class of carbon materials, carbon dots (CDs) obtained considerable funding originating from excellent luminescence characteristics, low cytotoxicity, stability, and easy preparation [15–18]. The CDs with peroxidase activity were used as nanozymes for application in chemosensing. Shamsipur group proposed that a strategy for sensing glutathione in human blood using CDs possessing peroxidase-like activity, which was synthesized by the pyrolysis of Na2EDTA·2H2O [19]. Subsequently, various CDs with peroxidase-like activity were prepared, respectively using raw materials folic acid, citric acid, thiourea, galactose and histidine [20–23]. In addition, metal doping in CDs was a simple and effective strategy to obtain excellent catalytic activity such as Fe [24], Mo [22], Cu [25], Mn [26]. Furthermore, excellent water solubility was also a colossal advantage of CDs nanozyme [27,28].

After the COVID-19 epidemic, family-stored outdated drugs might contaminate the environment. Additionally, illicit dealers would resell the expired medications to isolated locations, seriously impairing the ability of local patients to get care [29]. Therefore, “turning waste into wealth” was important when it comes to the creation of high value conversion technologies for pharmaceuticals that have expired. Furthermore, it may enrich the variety of CDs' synthetic raw materials, which aren't just restricted to small molecules and biological waste.

Motivated by the previously papers, a hydrothermal treatment of Fe(NO3)3 and expired amoxicillin—a widespread medication found in the family medical cabinet—was used to synthesize the Fe-doped carbon dots (Fe-CDs). Benefiting from the high peroxidase activity of Fe-CDs, an auto and precise colorimetric sensing strategy was established for TAC assay in human serum. What is more, the Fe-CDs could accelerate electron transfer and efficiently decomposing H2O2 to form·OH, which then catalyzes the colorless TMB to generate blue oxidized TMB (oxTMB). The antioxidant was added to the Fe-CDs/H2O2/TMB system could be identified because it inhibited the formation of oxTMB. Additionally, a recovery experiment was utilized to confirm the accuracy of our proposed assay for TAC, which was then applied to assess TAC in human serum.

Fe(NO3)3·9H2O, Co(NO3)2·6H2O, Cu(NO3)2·3H2O, NaAc, HAc, ascorbic acid(AsA) and potassium bromide were provided by KeLong Chemical Reagent Co. Ltd. (Chengdu, China). Dimethyl sulfoxide, Na2HPO4 and NaH2PO4 were purchased from JinShan Chemical Reagent Co. Ltd.(Chengdu, China). Fructose, threonine, sucrose, dopamine, glycine, glucose, protein, serine, H2O2(w = 30 %), glutathione(GSH), cysteine(Cys), 3,3,5,5 tetramethylbenzidine (TMB), 5, 5-Dimethyl-1-pyrroline N-oxide (DMPO) and N-Ethylmaleimide were purchase from Aladdin Co. Ltd. (Shanghai, China). The amoxil capsules from Harbin pharmaceutical general factory were valid until March 1, 2020. The ultrapure water were produced by Aike analysis equipment Co. Ltd. (Chengdu, China) and applied for all preparation aqueous reagents. The total antioxidant capacity assay kit was purchase from Beijing Boxbio Science ＆ Technology Co.,Ltd.

The UV–vis absorption spectra and absorbance were carried out using V-1800PC from Shanghai Mepinda Co., LTD. Fluorescence scanning of Fe-CDs was performed by F-4600 fluorescence spectrophotometer from Hitachi Co., LTD. The SY-01B integrated injection pump was used to control fluid distribution. The Fe-CDs of surface functional groups and element composition were analyzed by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy(K-Alpha, Thermo Scientific) and Fourier transform infrared spectrometerc(Spectrum-two-Li10014, Perkin Elmer). Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) photographs were recorded by a Tecnai G2 F20 transmission electron microscope (FEI, USA). The reaction process of Fe-CDs catalyzing H2O2 to form ·OH was monitored by electron paramagnetic resonance spectrometer(200 M, Guoyi Quantum Technology Co., Ltd).

A typical hydrothermal method was applied to synthesize M−CDs (M = Fe, Cu, Co). In brief, the expired amoxicillin (0.25 g) was transferred to 20 mL of pure water until completely dissolved. Subsequently, an additional precursor, namely metal nitrate, specifically Cu(NO3)2·3H2O (0.0087 g), Fe(NO3)9H2O (0.1454 g) and Co(NO3)2·6H2O (0.1484 g), was added into the aforementioned mixture. Then, the mixed solution was transferred to a Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave at 140 °C for 6 h, facilitating the synthesis of metal-doped carbon dots (M−CDs). Finally, the obtained M−CDs were subjected to centrifuge at 8000 rpm for 20 min to eliminate incomplete particles. Subsequently, the resulting supernatant diluted to a final volume of 50 mL and stored at 4℃, with a concentration of 5 mg·mL−1.

The integration of nanozymes-mediated catalytic reactions into flow analysis holds great potential for enabling rapid and precise detection of TAC [30]. The developed integrated sequential injection spectrophotometric system (iSIS) consisted of a 7-port selection valve equipped with a 2.5 mL injection syringe (see Fig. 1 ). The detection system consisted of a spectrophotometer equipped with a universal flow cell. The connection between the integrated injection syringe and reservoir holder rack, as well as between that and the detection system, was established using a PTFE pipeline with an inner diameter of 5 mm. The response signals were recorded through computer-based data collection.

The design strategy of iSIS employed by the established automatic analysis system involved sequential passage of samples and reagents through the selector valve, facilitating their mixing and reaction within the syringe. The reaction product was subsequently introduced into the flow cell for spectrophotometric analysis. The Fe-CDs/H2O2/TMB catalytic reaction was implemented by priming the system, which involved filling the tube connected to the reservoir holder rack and syringe pump. The analytical procedure for determining total antioxidant capacity (TAC) using the iSIS instrument was presented in Table S1.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Enzyme-like catalytic property of the Me-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: The POD-like catalytic property of Me-CDs was assessed using a validated spectrophotometric method based on TMB/H2O2 system. Typically, 100 μL of Me-CDs (0.5 mg/mL) were individually added to a mixed solution consisting of 1.8 mL NaAc-HAc buffer (pH 4), 100 μL TMB (5 mg/mL), and 200 μL H2O2 (30 %). The reaction solution mentioned above was rapidly mixed and incubated for a duration of 10 min. Subsequently, the absorbance at 652 nm and corresponding absorption curves were meticulously recorded using an UV–vis spectrophotometer. Moreover, to confirm the oxidase-like activity (OXD-like) of Me-CDs, the buffer solution of equal volume was used instead of H2O2 while keeping all other procedures.

The hydrothermal synthesis of Me-CDs (Me = Cu, Co, Fe) was conducted by incorporating metallic nitrate into expired amoxicillin. The POD-like and OXD-like activities of Me-CDs (0.5 mg/mL) were investigated using the TMB (5 mg/mL) catalytic oxidation reaction system, with 30 % of H2O2 serving as the POD substrate. The reaction time was 5 min, and the absorption spectra were recorded in Fig. 2 . With the incorporation of Me-CDs, the absorption spectra of the TMB/H2O2 solution and TMB alone exhibited a distinctive absorption peak at 652 nm, which can be attributed to the oxidation process of TMB. The results indicated that Me-CDs exhibit POD-like (Fig. 2A) and OXD-like (Fig. 2B) activities. Furthermore, Fe-CDs demonstrated superior POD-like and OXD-like activities, as evidenced by the higher absorption intensity at 652 nm.

The kinetic parameters of Fe-CDs as peroxide mimics were extensively investigated from the perspective theory. In this catalytic experiment, the kinetic parameters (K m and V m) can be determined through a double reciprocal plot obtained by varying the concentration one substrate while keeping the other substrate fixed. Equation of double reciprocal curve:

where V was determined by analyzing the kinetic curve of TMB catalytic oxidation reaction (ΔA/Δt5min). [S] was the concentration of substrate, and K m was a constant that evaluates the affinity between enzyme to substrate. By fitting initial reaction velocity values and the concentration of substrate, the Lineweaver-Burk plot (Figs. S1 and S2) were accomplished. The K m of Fe-CDs and Co-CDs using TMB as a substrate were 0.028 mM and 2.08 mM, respectively. The data presented in Table S2 clearly demonstrate that Fe-CDs exhibit the lowest K m value, thereby indicating their superior affinity towards TMB. The possible explanation lies in the structural similarity between Fe-doped CDs and the porphyrin structure found in natural peroxidase.

Therefore, Fe-CDs was selected as the peroxidase mimic for further investigation due to their superior enzymatic activity. Moreover, Fe-CDs exhibited optimal peroxidase and oxidase activity at a pH of 3.6 in the catalytic reaction environment (Fig. 2C). The results demonstrated that the pH of the catalytic reaction played a crucial role in regulating both the catalytic activity and selectivity. The data and discussion on the structural characterization of Fe-CDs were placed in supplementary material (Fig. S3–S5).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of reducing substance on Fe-CDs/H2O2/TMB reaction system
ce:section.ce:para: The oxidation of TMB by H2O2 was catalyzed by Fe-CDs, which exhibited inherent peroxide-like activity. Furthermore, a comparison of the XPS fine spectra of Fe before and after the catalytic reaction reveals no change in the valence state of Fe (see Fig. S6), thereby indicating that Fe-CDs function as an effective catalyst.

Subsequently, three representative reducing agents, namely AsA, GSH and Cys, were introduced into the catalytic reaction Fe-CDs/H2O2/TMB to assess their impact on the absorption spectral curves depicted in Fig. 3 A. The characteristic absorption of the mixed liquid at 652 nm exhibited a significant decrease in the presence of typical reducing substances. Among them, the inhibitory effect of AsA on the formation of oxTMB was the highest and the greatest degree of fading. Therefore, AsA was chosen as a representative molecule to assess the capability of the Fe-CDs/H2O2/TMB catalytic reaction system for TAC detection. Electron spin resonance (ESR) spectroscopy was employed to confirm the presence of reactive intermediates in POD-like catalysis and their elimination by AsA (Fig. 3B). Quadruple signal corresponding to hydroxyl radicals (1:2:2:1) was exclusively observed upon mixing Fe-CDs with H2O2. Subsequently, different concentrations of AsA were introduced into the Fe-CDs/H2O2 mixture, resulting in suppression or even loss of the characteristic quadruple signal. Consequently, the POD-like activity of Fe-CDs was attributed to their catalytic decomposition of H2O2, thereby inducing the generation of hydroxyl radicals. Moreover, the removal of intermediate reaction products constituted the underlying mechanism for AsA inhibition on the Fe-CDs/H2O2/TMB system.

To further investigate the fading effect of AsA on oxTMB, this fading reaction mechanism was evaluated by a recirculating flow analysis system, which enabled real-time monitoring of catalytic reactions upon addition of other molecules (Fig. S7A [25]. The absorbance at 652 nm of the catalytic reaction system exhibits a significant decrease in the presence of varying concentrations of AsA, as depicted in Fig. S7B. Subsequently, a reformation of the catalytic color product occurs after a certain period of time. Notably, it is worth highlighting that the duration for fading is positively associated with the concentration of AsA. The slope of the linear correlation curve for AsA remained consistent across different fading time conditions, indicating that the sensitivity of AsA determination is minimally affected by fading time (Fig. S8). Accurate control over fading time could potentially enhance the accuracy of AsA detection.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analytical performance of Fe-CDs nanozyme for the detection of H2O2 and AsA
ce:section.ce:para: The Fe-CDs/H2O2/TMB/AsA catalytic reaction system was incorporated into the sequential injection system, leveraging the specific peroxidase-like activity of Fe-CDs and the fading properties of AsA. The initial condition were as follows: HAc-NaAc buffer solution (pH 3.6) was 1 mL; the concentration of TMB (200 μL) was 0.5 g·L−1; the concentration of Fe-CDs (200 μL) was 0.1 mg·mL−1; the concentration of H2O2 (400 μL) was 5 mM; the detection wavelength was 652 nm. The influence factors affecting the analytical performance of TAC, including the concentration of Fe-CDs, H2O2, TMB, flow rate, and fading time were individually investigated in the iSIS system to achieve satisfactory results. As depicted in Fig. S9, considering both linearity and sensitivity, the optimal conditions for this study were determined as follows: 0.5 g·L−1 for TMB concentration; 0.25 mg·mL−1 for Fe-CDs concentration; and 5 mM for H2O2 concentration.

As an antioxidant, AsA effectively inhibits the POD activity of Fe-CDs and alters the color of oxTMB. To ensure precision, 3 mg·L−1 AsA was injected into the iSIS system for a total of 11 times under optimized conditions. The experimental results were presented in Fig. S10, demonstrating that the response peak of AsA at the same concentration remained essentially unchanged. Additionally, the relative standard deviation (RSD) was calculated to be less than 1.12 %, confirming the satisfactory repeatability of the designed iSIS system. Subsequently, various concentrations of AsA standard solutions were injected into the iSIS system to assess its linear range for AsA (Fig. 4 ). The linear range of the assay spanned from 0.5 to 15 mg·L−1, yielding a linear fitting equation of ΔA = 0.0411 CAsA + 0.0505 (r = 0.9993). The limit of detection (LOD) for AsA was determined to be as low as 0.13 mg·L−1 (3.3 s/k), which is comparable or even lower than that reported in other peroxidase nanozyme-based assays (Table S3). Furthermore, based on this method, the concentrations of reductive molecules (GSH and Cys) similar to AsA could also be accurately quantified using the following linear equations: ΔA = 0.001 CGSH + 0.007 (10–50 mg·L−1) and ΔA = 0.0028 CCys 0.0218 (3–50 mg·L−1) (Fig. S11).

To further investigate the selectivity of the Fe-CDs simulated peroxidase system for TAC detection, various common potential interferences present in human serum, including metal ions (Na+, K+, Ca2+, Mg2+), sulfite, nitrite, glucose, arginine, proline, histidine, cysteine, and BSA were introduced into the reaction system(Fig. S12). Despite exhibiting higher concentrations compared to AsA, Cys, and GSH, the response intensity of iSIS remained unaltered, thus demonstrating minimal interference effects on TAC analysis when employed for detection purposes.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Application of the iSIS system to detect TAC based on the Fe-CDs nanozyme
ce:section.ce:para: To validate the applicability of proposed iSIS system based on the Fe-CDs nanozyme to detect TAC in practical applications. Human serum samples from individuals with normal, diabetic, and hypertensive conditions were chosen as authentic specimens. These human serum samples were centrifuged for 30 min and subsequently diluted by a factor of 10 with distilled water. Subsequently, they were injected into the iSIS system for the detection of (TAC). The experimental results obtained were shown in Table 1 , there was little difference between the proposed method and the standard method.

And then, recovery experiments were conducted by adding various concentrations of AsA standard solutions into three human serum samples, aiming to validate the accuracy of the method. The addition of N-Ethylmaleimide (0.3 mM) to the real samples was performed as a means to eliminate interference from GSH and Cys, thus ensuring the accuracy of the analysis [31]. The recovery rates of AsA in serum ranged from 97.60 % to 105.33 %, as shown in Table 2 . Therefore, the results obtained using the presented method were deemed accurate and reliable.

In summary, the expired amoxicillin was successfully transformed into Fe-CDs nanozymes with peroxide-like activity, demonstrating remarkable efficacy in decomposing H2O2 into ·OH radicals. Undoubtedly, this innovative approach presents an intriguing solution for managing expired pharmaceuticals and facilitating. Subsequently, the addition of varying concentrations of antioxidants resulted in a reduction in absorbance of the Fe-CDs/H2O2/TMB system, as observed from the AsA inhibition of Fe-CDs catalyzed oxidation of TMB. Therefore, with the help of sequential injection technique, an automatic and accurate analysis method for TAC detection was established using AsA as the antioxidant model. This method exhibits a wide linear range of 0.5 to 15 mg·L−1 and a lower limit of detection. Furthermore, this assay has been utilized to detect TAC levels in human serum.

Guo-Qi Zhang: Conceptualization, Data curation, Writing – review & editing. Wei-ping Liu: Data curation, Funding acquisition. Yu-Han Shi: Investigation, Writing – original draft. Yu-Xian Zou: Investigation, Writing – original draft. Li-Yao Xu: Investigation. Hui-Ling Cheng: Investigation. Tao Wang: Investigation. Wen-Bin Li: Investigation. Yan Zhao: Methodology. Zhi-Hong Xu: Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We appreciate the financial support from the Sichuan Science and Technology Program No. 2023NSFSC0637 and Xihua University Talent Introduction Program (No. RX2200001988). We would like to thank Shiyanjia Lab (www.shiyanjia.com) for the XPS and TEM testing and analysis.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.microc.2023.109720.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Aging and injury: alterations in cellular energetics and organ function

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: N. Poulose, R. Raju, Aging and injury: alterations in cellular energetics and organ function, Aging. Dis. 5 (2014) 101-108.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Role of oxidative stress in Alzheimer’s disease

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: W.J. Huang, X. Zhang, W.W. Chen, Role of oxidative stress in Alzheimer’s disease, Biomed. Rep. 4 (2016) 519-522.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A polymetallic nanozyme with high peroxidase mimetic activity for rapid evaluation of total antioxidant capacity

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: J. Dan, Z.H. Su, B.Y. Sun, J.L. Wang, W.T. Zhang, A polymetallic nanozyme with high peroxidase mimetic activity for rapid evaluation of total antioxidant capacity, Microchem. J., 185 (2023) 108302.
G. Perry, A.D. Cash, M.A. Smith, Alzheimer disease and oxidative stress, J. Biomed. Biotechnol. 2 (2002) 120-123.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Standardized methods for the determination of antioxidant capacity and phenolics in foods and dietary supplements

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: R.L. Prior, X. Wu, K. Schaich, Standardized methods for the determination of antioxidant capacity and phenolics in foods and dietary supplements, J. Agric. Food Chem. 53 (2005) 4290-4302.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Antioxidants and antioxidant methods: an updated overview

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: L. Gulcin, Antioxidants and antioxidant methods: an updated overview, Arch. Toxicol. 94 (2020) 651-715.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Advantages and limitations of common testing methods for antioxidants

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: R. Amorati, L. Valgimigli, Advantages and limitations of common testing methods for antioxidants, Free Radic. Res. 49 (2015) 633-649.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Intrinsic peroxidase-like activity of ferromagnetic nanoparticles

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: L. Gao, J. Zhuang, L. Nie, J. Zhang, Y. Zhang, N. Gu, T. Wang, J. Feng, D. Yang, S. Perrett, X.Y. Yan, Intrinsic peroxidase-like activity of ferromagnetic nanoparticles, Nat. Nanotechnol. 9 (2007) 577-583.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Modulation of the biocatalytic activity and selectivity of CeO2 nanozymes via atomic doping engineering

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: S.F. Zhang, H.Y. Ruan, Q. Xin, X.Y. Mu, H. Wang, X.D. Zhang, Modulation of the biocatalytic activity and selectivity of CeO2 nanozymes via atomic doping engineering, Nanoscale 15 (2023) 4408-4419.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Light-responsive Au nanoclusters with oxidase-like activity for fluorescent detection of total antioxidant capacity
